supporting_evidence.snippet:
Image 9: Zynap
Zynap, a Barcelona-based cybersecurity startup leveraging Gen-AI to fight cybercrime proactively by simulating cyber threat tactics, has announced its launch and close of their €5.7 million funding round to fuel their expansion plans.
The round was co-led by Kibo Ventures and Kfund, including key business angles. Together, they have provided the necessary capital to accelerate Zynap’s technology​ development and expansion plans.
“_Our mission is simple yet ambitious: Outsmarting Cybercrime. We believe cybercrime is a challenge that cannot be tackled with generic solutions but requires advanced technology, reliable data, and a disruptive approach_,” said Founder and CEO Daniel Solís. [...] Zynap’s team is currently developing a trial version of its product, set to be released in the coming months.

Zynap aims to redefine Threat Intelligence through technology that delivers actionable and hard-to-access operational insights. Its solutions empower enterprises and MSSPs (Managed Security Service Providers) to stay ahead of evolving digital threats. Zynap’s approach offers a practical, actionable focus, differentiating itself from existing market alternatives.

HomeFundingA new line of defense: Cybersecurity startup Zynap raises €5.7 million for...
FundingSpain-Startups
A new line of defense: Cybersecurity startup Zynap raises €5.7 million for threat intelligence
Image 8: David Cendon Garcia
ByDavid Cendon Garcia
January 21, 2025
Share
FacebookTwitterWhatsAppLinkedin

Image 9: Zynap
Zynap, a Barcelona-based cybersecurity startup leveraging Gen-AI to fight cybercrime proactively by simulating cyber threat tactics, has announced its launch and close of their €5.7 million funding round to fuel their expansion plans.
The round was co-led by Kibo Ventures and Kfund, including key business angles. Together, they have provided the necessary capital to accelerate Zynap’s technology​ development and expansion plans.
“_Our mission is simple yet ambitious: Outsmarting Cybercrime. We believe cybercrime is a challenge that cannot be tackled with generic solutions but requires advanced technology, reliable data, and a disruptive approach_,” said Founder and CEO Daniel Solís.
Founded in 2024 by Daniel Solís, Zynap benefits from his experience as former CEO and founder of Blueliv and the backing of advisors like Pedro Castillo, founder of Onum and Devo, Spain’s first cybersecurity unicorn.
Zynap aims to redefine Threat Intelligence through technology that delivers actionable and hard-to-access operational insights. Its solutions empower enterprises and MSSPs (Managed Security Service Providers) to stay ahead of evolving digital threats. Zynap’s approach offers a practical, actionable focus, differentiating itself from existing market alternatives.
“_We’ve known the team for over a decade, and it’s inspiring to see them come together again, stronger and more experienced, around such a distinctive and essential product for the cybersecurity industry. We’re thrilled to invest in this team and their new venture for the second time_,” says Juan Santamaría, partner at Kibo Ventures.
“_We’re investing in Zynap because we see an extraordinary team with a clear vision and a product that solves real problems for large enterprises and MSSPs. We’re excited to be part of this journey,_” adds Jaime Novoa, partner at Kfund.
Zynap is designed to meet the needs of MSSPs and Fortune 2000 companies seeking agile, scalable, and effective tools to defend against cyberattacks. In a landscape where threats constantly evolve, Zynap looks to deliver innovative, market-differentiating solutions.
Its standardised solutions ensure integration with existing tools and frameworks while offering customisation to suit specific client and sector needs. Zynap provides measurable outcomes that empower clients to make informed decisions.
Additionally, by leveraging generative AI alongside other advanced technologies, Zynap reportedly delivers precise results that enable businesses to protect themselves from cybercriminal threats even before they occur.
Zynap’s team is currently developing a trial version of its product, set to be released in the coming months.

supporting_evidence.raw_content:
Logo
Logo
Logo

Logo
Barcelona-based Genie snaps €1.1 million to empower data science teams with its no-code app builder
Stefano De Marzo

Barcelona-based Genie, an open-source startup behind the web framework for the fast-growing Julia programming language, announced that it has raised a €1.1 million pre-seed round led by Speedinvest, with participation from Expa Ventures, Antler, APX/Heartfelt, and Shine Capital. The funds are being used to advance the development of Genie Builder, its no-code web app builder for data science and R&D teams.
Genie empowers data scientists, researchers and engineers to rapidly turn their data analysis code, machine learning models and simulations into shareable web applications. With Genie’s low/no-code web development tools, they can build any kind of data-centric web applications, from analytics dashboards to AI prototypes and products, simulation apps and smart tools, driving business impact faster and at a fraction of the cost of traditional software development.
“In today’s enterprise, virtually every department needs to build custom web-based tools and products, including teams in data science and R&D. At Genie, we empower this diverse group of data professionals, engineers, scientists and researchers to share their work easily with web apps and amplify their impact within their organizations,” said Genie’s co-founder Cinzia Palumbo. 
Founded in 2021, Genie offers a comprehensive suite of full-code, low-code and no-code tools for web app development specially designed for Julia, a high-performance computing language with over 1 million active users. Its open-source Genie Framework has been downloaded over 90,000  times and has been starred over 2,000 times by developers on GitHub. The framework is widely used by data scientists, researchers, and engineers in computationally demanding industries, such as life sciences, financial services, manufacturing, aerospace, IT consulting, and more.
The company unveiled a no-code tool, Genie Builder, which allows users to quickly create web apps around ML models, simulations, and data analysis code. Genie Builder launched in 2022 and has been accumulating over 2,500 users.
“Genie users are building a wide variety of applications including data exploration dashboards, financial data products, ML/AI, and simulation apps, bioinformatics apps, internal tools and more. With our no-code Genie Builder, they can build these data products in hours, instead of days and weeks, at a fraction of the cost it’d take had they engaged a software development team,” added Adrian Salceanu, co-founder and creator of the Genie Framework.
With Gartner projecting that 70% of new enterprise applications will leverage low-code or no-code technologies by 2025, up from under 25% in 2020, the market is ripe for growth. Genie plans to use the funds to evolve Genie Builder into a next-gen no-code platform by incorporating generative AI for custom code generation, styling automation, and more. The company also plans to develop Genie Builder into a polyglot platform supporting multiple data science languages including Python, and adding the capability to deploy and run web apps in one-click.
“Genie’s tools streamline the development of custom web apps for data science and R&D teams,” commented Yang Tran, investor at Speedinvest. “The recent inclusion of Julia in the Tiobe top 20 programming languages index underscores the importance of companies like Genie. With their impressive user growth and unwavering dedication to serving the data science and the research and development communities, we are confident in Genie’s ability to make a significant impact on the no-code market.”
Stefano De Marzo

HomeFundingMadrid's Anyformat closes €3.3 million round to scale its agentic OCR and...
FundingSpain-Startups
Madrid’s Anyformat closes €3.3 million round to scale its agentic OCR and data-sovereignty-focused document tools
Image 8: David Cendon Garcia
ByDavid Cendon Garcia
November 27, 2025
Share
FacebookTwitterWhatsAppLinkedin

Image 9: Anyformat
Anyformat, a Spanish GenAI platform specialising in extracting and structuring complex data from any document, has closed a Seed round of €3.3 million to expand the engineering and product team, driving its roadmap in advanced extraction capabilities and the development of proprietary error detection models.
The round was led by Kibo Ventures and followed by 4Founders, Abac Nest Ventures and Decelera Ventures, funds that already participated in the previous round.
Juan Huguet, CEO and co-founder of Anyformat, says, “_Documents are the backbone of any organisation. Our goal is to enable companies to analyse them with human intelligence, machine speed and the security required by regulation. We are building the new European document infrastructure.‘_(Translated).
Among European startups working on document processing, data extraction and AI-enabled enterprise automation, 2025 funding activity has been limited but illustrative: France-based Klaimy raised €1.2 million to advance its AI assistant for processing medical documentation, while Prague-headquartered Resistant AI secured €21 million in Series B financing to strengthen its document-fraud detection and financial-crime prevention systems.
Together, these comparable 2025 rounds amount to roughly €22.2 million, indicating that investor attention in this domain remains focused on specialised applications such as insurance, medical underwriting and fraud detection rather than broad, general-purpose document-infrastructure platforms.
In this context, Anyformat’s €3.3 million Seed roundpositions it as one of the few European companies pursuing a horizontal, generative-AI-based document-intelligence layer – particularly notable given the absence of other Spain-based fundraises in the category – adding meaningful weight to the still-emerging European document-automation landscape.
Diego Pérez, co-founder and CTO of the company, adds:_“We are developing technology that goes far beyond document digitisation_.” (Translated)
Founded in 2024, Anyformat is innovating a new generation of document infrastructure based on generative AI, which reportedly allows the operational teams of large corporations to work with complex documents with human precision and machine speed, while maintaining control of the data at all times.
Since its previous round, where it raised €520k in pre-Seed funding (as covered by EU-Startups), Anyformat has expanded its offering beyond classic extraction. Today, the company says they are the only provider that allows the information extracted from a document to be combined in real time with internal and proprietary company data.
This means that a contract, invoice or email can be analysed automatically while being cross-referenced directly with corporate databases, such as customers, policies, catalogues or history.
For organisations with complex documentation and fragmented systems, this capability eliminates manual validations, reduces errors and accelerates critical business processes.
This represents a real problem: according to Dell Technologies’ Innovation Catalyst report, 68% of Spanish companies are unable to convert their data into valuable information in real time.
“_The document intelligence market is entering a new phase driven by generative AI and agentic systems. Anyformat has developed a unique technology that allows companies to automate critical processes while maintaining data sovereignty, a key issue in Europe._
_“The vision and talent of the founders, whom we know from their time at Clarity.ai, a company also invested in by Kibo Ventures, combined with the possibilities offered by AI in a huge market, were key factors in our decision to invest_,” shares Javier Torremocha, co-founder and Managing Partner of Kibo Ventures.
Anyformat aims to exceed €1 million in ARR in 2026, supported by its commercial expansion in Europe and the increase in use cases linked to automation, compliance and advanced document processing.
In addition, the company will continue to invest in its privacy-first infrastructure, strengthening security processes and advancing certifications such as ISO 27001.
The company is positioning its Agentic OCR as the new standard for automating critical processes in organisations that cannot compromise on accuracy or data control.
   Tags

   anyformat
   decelera ventures
   Dell Technologies

supporting_evidence.title: Spanish government raises €67 million for Multiverse ...
supporting_evidence.snippet:
Logo
Spanish government raises €67 million for Multiverse Computing for AI compression
David Cendon Garcia
Multiverse-Computing
Spanish Minister for Digital Transformation and Public Function, Óscar López, announced on Tuesday that the Government of Spain will become a shareholder of the Donostia-based company Multiverse Computing, with a co-investment of €67 million.
The operation will be carried out through the Spanish Society for Technological Transformation (SETT).
Rodrigo Hernandez, Global GenAI Director at Multiverse Computing, will also be attending the EU-Startups Summit 2025 in Malta as one of our Speakers, read more here. [...] Their other product CompactifAI, is an AI model compression tool that reduces AI model size while maintaining performance, leading to lower energy consumption and costs. CompactifAI is meant for real-time processing and edge computing applications. So far, it has been adopted by enterprises in healthcare, automotive, and telecommunications.
“With this operation, we seek to position Spain as a benchmark in energy-efficient AI language models, scaling the development of compaction tools that compress language models by up to 90%, reducing costs and energy consumption while increasing the technological independence of Spain and Europe,” said Minister López at a press conference in the Spanish Pavilion at MWC Barcelona 2025. [...] “We appreciate the support of the Spanish government and will work to make Spain and Europe leaders in this technology. Also, support from the Basque government has helped us achieve the success we have today,” said Enrique Lizaso Olmos, Co-founder and CEO of Multiverse Computing. “We are guiding the next generation of AI algorithms by making these models smaller and more efficient to lower operating costs and reduce the environmental impact.”
Founded in 2019 by Enrique Lizaso Olmos, Román Orús, Samuel Mugel, and Alfonso Rubio, Multiverse Computing has developed software inspired by quantum computing that allows AI models to be compressed to 10% of the original size, consuming fewer resources and obtaining equivalent returns.

David Cendon Garcia
Multiverse-Computing
Spanish Minister for Digital Transformation and Public Function, Óscar López, announced on Tuesday that the Government of Spain will become a shareholder of the Donostia-based company Multiverse Computing, with a co-investment of €67 million.
The operation will be carried out through the Spanish Society for Technological Transformation (SETT).
Rodrigo Hernandez, Global GenAI Director at Multiverse Computing, will also be attending the EU-Startups Summit 2025 in Malta as one of our Speakers, read more here.
“We appreciate the support of the Spanish government and will work to make Spain and Europe leaders in this technology. Also, support from the Basque government has helped us achieve the success we have today,” said Enrique Lizaso Olmos, Co-founder and CEO of Multiverse Computing. “We are guiding the next generation of AI algorithms by making these models smaller and more efficient to lower operating costs and reduce the environmental impact.”
Founded in 2019 by Enrique Lizaso Olmos, Román Orús, Samuel Mugel, and Alfonso Rubio, Multiverse Computing has developed software inspired by quantum computing that allows AI models to be compressed to 10% of the original size, consuming fewer resources and obtaining equivalent returns.
This allows, for example, to use these models using less energy or on smaller devices, such as virtual reality glasses or smartphones instead of large computers, even in offline mode.
Their mission is to apply quantum and quantum-inspired AI to solve complex problems in finance, energy, manufacturing, defense, cybersecurity, life sciences, and chemistry, delivering practical applications and tangible value.
They look to provide efficient software to companies wanting to gain an edge with quantum computing and AI.
One of their flagship products is Singularity, a software platform that contains quantum and quantum-inspired algorithms developed and patented through proof-of-concept trials we have been performing for industrial and service clients.
Their other product CompactifAI, is an AI model compression tool that reduces AI model size while maintaining performance, leading to lower energy consumption and costs. CompactifAI is meant for real-time processing and edge computing applications. So far, it has been adopted by enterprises in healthcare, automotive, and telecommunications.
“With this operation, we seek to position Spain as a benchmark in energy-efficient AI language models, scaling the development of compaction tools that compress language models by up to 90%, reducing costs and energy consumption while increasing the technological independence of Spain and Europe,” said Minister López at a press conference in the Spanish Pavilion at MWC Barcelona 2025.
The Minister also announced new grants aimed at helping companies incorporate AI into their value chains. Firstly, a new call for €130 million from ERDF funds. The grants, of up to €5 million per project, will be articulated through the Red.es entity and “will allow our companies to launch experimental development projects in which intensive use will be made of technologies associated with AI, such as machine learning and deep learning, generative AI or LLMs, among others.”
In addition, Minister López announced another call for €50 million euros for the integration of AI in companies in the health sector; both, added to the call to promote the use of AI and data in Spanish SMEs, closed a few days ago and which has a budget of €24 million, “are more than €200 million of European funds–ERDF and RTRP–that will be activated in the coming days and that will allow us to continue exploiting our strengths in the field of Artificial Intelligence,” said the Minister.
López made these announcements at MWC Barcelona 2025 during his speech at the event ‘CambIA: from research to business,’ in which some of the results of the ‘R+D Missions in AI’ programme were announced. This initiative, endowed with €50 million from the RTRP and launched by the Secretary of State for Digitalisation and Artificial Intelligence (SEDIA) in 2021, concluded last December and has developed real use cases for the application of AI in strategic sectors of the country’s economy.
David Cendon Garcia

Reputational harm keeps them up at night,” said Ariadna Font Llitjós, co-founder & CEO of Alinia. “Using a process known as Alignment, we ensure AI systems adhere to expected behaviors, policies, and regulatory requirements – helping enterprises seize the promise of this new technology while minimizing the risks and potential harm.”
supporting_evidence.raw_content:
Logo
Logo
Logo

Logo
Barcelona-based Alinia raises €2.2 million pre-seed to enable safe deployment of genAI
Stefano De Marzo

Alinia AI, an alignment platform enabling companies’ safe and controlled deployment of generative AI, guided by their policies and business preferences, announces a successful €2.2 million pre-seed funding round. Speedinvest and Precursor led the round with participation from KFund and notable angels including Clem Délangue & Thom Wolf (Hugging Face Co-Founders), Tom Preston (GitHub Co-Founder), Xavier Amatriain (Google Core AI VP), and Oriol Vinyals (Google DeepMind VP of Research).
Significant adoption of generative AI is underway with spending set to hit $143 billion by 2027, but its rapid adoption comes with significant challenges. The problem? Managing control and safety in the face of risks to prevent inappropriate or brand-damaging content. Additionally, navigating upcoming regulatory efforts like the EU AI Act, Biden´s Executive Order, or Canada´s Bill C-27, which demand rigorous safety checks and clear documentation, also adds hurdles to mass enterprise adoption. 
Evaluating the performance and reliability of generative AI applications from both safety and business dimensions is a top obstacle for the enterprise today. This is especially challenging for companies when leveraging advanced techniques like fine-tuning and RAG, maintaining LLM versions, and coordinating across teams including machine learning, governance, and business domain experts.
“The technology is not production-ready for business scenarios. Enterprise leaders are rightfully afraid of making headlines because their generative AI applications go rogue. Reputational harm keeps them up at night,” said Ariadna Font Llitjós, co-founder & CEO of Alinia. “Using a process known as Alignment, we ensure AI systems adhere to expected behaviors, policies, and regulatory requirements – helping enterprises seize the promise of this new technology while minimizing the risks and potential harm.”
Founded in 2023, Alinia Alignment Platform aims to provide an integral AI governance approach – which currently requires multiple tools – ranging from evaluation and real-time monitoring to cutting-edge optimization techniques, coupled with documentation features facilitating compliance at every stage of the LLM lifecycle. Enabling customers to have clear and accurate evidence of how LLM-powered applications behave in different enterprise scenarios, for specific tasks, and abiding by specific rules, is the first step towards alignment for the enterprise. 
“In my experience at Hugging Face, governance and safety aspects of LLM development require an incredible amount of time and effort. But when you are about to release a foundation model that will be used millions of times, governance and safety are non-negotiables,” addedCarlos Muñoz Ferrandis, co-founder & COO.
With an initial focus on enabling subject-matter experts to guide and validate the performance and safety of their generative AI applications, Alinia will use the funding to hire additional talent to build the Alinia Alignment Platform to create an end-to-end alignment process focused on safety and regulatory requirements –  enabling safe and inclusive use of generative AI across LLM modalities and languages. 
Fred Hagenauer, Partner with Speedinvest, said: “Ari and Carlos have some of the best profiles we´ve seen to tackle the alignment problem in the generative AI space. Their experience and knowledge of the early days of enterprise AI, RAI, and the  LLM governance required today is something truly unique. For us, it was a no-brainer to be the first ones to support them.”
Charles Hudson, Managing Partner with Precursor Ventures, commented: “Alinia brings the confidence that enterprise lacks today to deploy generative AI in a safe and trustworthy way. Being able to control and steer these powerful agents is such a challenge, but they have the experience needed and are willing to take it head-on.”
Stefano De Marzo

A new line of defense: Cybersecurity startup Zynap raises €5.7 million for threat intelligence
Image 8: David Cendon Garcia
ByDavid Cendon Garcia
January 21, 2025
Share
FacebookTwitterWhatsAppLinkedin

Image 9: Zynap
Zynap, a Barcelona-based cybersecurity startup leveraging Gen-AI to fight cybercrime proactively by simulating cyber threat tactics, has announced its launch and close of their €5.7 million funding round to fuel their expansion plans.
The round was co-led by Kibo Ventures and Kfund, including key business angles. Together, they have provided the necessary capital to accelerate Zynap’s technology​ development and expansion plans.
“_Our mission is simple yet ambitious: Outsmarting Cybercrime. We believe cybercrime is a challenge that cannot be tackled with generic solutions but requires advanced technology, reliable data, and a disruptive approach_,” said Founder and CEO Daniel Solís.
Founded in 2024 by Daniel Solís, Zynap benefits from his experience as former CEO and founder of Blueliv and the backing of advisors like Pedro Castillo, founder of Onum and Devo, Spain’s first cybersecurity unicorn.
Zynap aims to redefine Threat Intelligence through technology that delivers actionable and hard-to-access operational insights. Its solutions empower enterprises and MSSPs (Managed Security Service Providers) to stay ahead of evolving digital threats. Zynap’s approach offers a practical, actionable focus, differentiating itself from existing market alternatives.
“_We’ve known the team for over a decade, and it’s inspiring to see them come together again, stronger and more experienced, around such a distinctive and essential product for the cybersecurity industry. We’re thrilled to invest in this team and their new venture for the second time_,” says Juan Santamaría, partner at Kibo Ventures.
“_We’re investing in Zynap because we see an extraordinary team with a clear vision and a product that solves real problems for large enterprises and MSSPs. We’re excited to be part of this journey,_” adds Jaime Novoa, partner at Kfund.
Zynap is designed to meet the needs of MSSPs and Fortune 2000 companies seeking agile, scalable, and effective tools to defend against cyberattacks. In a landscape where threats constantly evolve, Zynap looks to deliver innovative, market-differentiating solutions.
Its standardised solutions ensure integration with existing tools and frameworks while offering customisation to suit specific client and sector needs. Zynap provides measurable outcomes that empower clients to make informed decisions.
Additionally, by leveraging generative AI alongside other advanced technologies, Zynap reportedly delivers precise results that enable businesses to protect themselves from cybercriminal threats even before they occur.
Zynap’s team is currently developing a trial version of its product, set to be released in the coming months.

supporting_evidence.raw_content:
Logo
Logo
Logo

Logo
Barcelona-based Genie snaps €1.1 million to empower data science teams with its no-code app builder
Stefano De Marzo

Barcelona-based Genie, an open-source startup behind the web framework for the fast-growing Julia programming language, announced that it has raised a €1.1 million pre-seed round led by Speedinvest, with participation from Expa Ventures, Antler, APX/Heartfelt, and Shine Capital. The funds are being used to advance the development of Genie Builder, its no-code web app builder for data science and R&D teams.
Genie empowers data scientists, researchers and engineers to rapidly turn their data analysis code, machine learning models and simulations into shareable web applications. With Genie’s low/no-code web development tools, they can build any kind of data-centric web applications, from analytics dashboards to AI prototypes and products, simulation apps and smart tools, driving business impact faster and at a fraction of the cost of traditional software development.
“In today’s enterprise, virtually every department needs to build custom web-based tools and products, including teams in data science and R&D. At Genie, we empower this diverse group of data professionals, engineers, scientists and researchers to share their work easily with web apps and amplify their impact within their organizations,” said Genie’s co-founder Cinzia Palumbo. 
Founded in 2021, Genie offers a comprehensive suite of full-code, low-code and no-code tools for web app development specially designed for Julia, a high-performance computing language with over 1 million active users. Its open-source Genie Framework has been downloaded over 90,000  times and has been starred over 2,000 times by developers on GitHub. The framework is widely used by data scientists, researchers, and engineers in computationally demanding industries, such as life sciences, financial services, manufacturing, aerospace, IT consulting, and more.
The company unveiled a no-code tool, Genie Builder, which allows users to quickly create web apps around ML models, simulations, and data analysis code. Genie Builder launched in 2022 and has been accumulating over 2,500 users.
“Genie users are building a wide variety of applications including data exploration dashboards, financial data products, ML/AI, and simulation apps, bioinformatics apps, internal tools and more. With our no-code Genie Builder, they can build these data products in hours, instead of days and weeks, at a fraction of the cost it’d take had they engaged a software development team,” added Adrian Salceanu, co-founder and creator of the Genie Framework.
With Gartner projecting that 70% of new enterprise applications will leverage low-code or no-code technologies by 2025, up from under 25% in 2020, the market is ripe for growth. Genie plans to use the funds to evolve Genie Builder into a next-gen no-code platform by incorporating generative AI for custom code generation, styling automation, and more. The company also plans to develop Genie Builder into a polyglot platform supporting multiple data science languages including Python, and adding the capability to deploy and run web apps in one-click.
“Genie’s tools streamline the development of custom web apps for data science and R&D teams,” commented Yang Tran, investor at Speedinvest. “The recent inclusion of Julia in the Tiobe top 20 programming languages index underscores the importance of companies like Genie. With their impressive user growth and unwavering dedication to serving the data science and the research and development communities, we are confident in Genie’s ability to make a significant impact on the no-code market.”
Stefano De Marzo

Spanish Minister for Digital Transformation and Public Function, Óscar López, announced on Tuesday that the Government of Spain will become a shareholder of the Donostia-based company Multiverse Computing, with a co-investment of €67 million.

HomeFundingMadrid-based startup Shakers raises €14 million to help businesses integrate teams of...
FundingSpain-Startups
Madrid-based startup Shakers raises €14 million to help businesses integrate teams of specialised talent with AI
By David Cendon Garcia
Share
FacebookTwitterWhatsAppLinkedin
HRTech startup Shakers, a Spanish workforce management platform connecting companies with tech freelancers, today announced its €14 million Series A funding round in order to expand double its headcount.
The funding round was led by Partech, with further investment from KFund, Brighteye Ventures, Athos Capital and Wayra.
Héctor Mata, Shakers CEO, said: “The convergence of AI and independent talent is not a trend – it’s a new operating model. Demand for specialised technical talent is soaring, but the traditional outsourcing model hasn’t evolved in over decades. With Shakers, we are setting a new standard for hiring: our platform acts as the new operating system for performance-driven freelancers — giving them the same benefits as traditional roles, while offering companies the agility to innovate faster. As we expand across Europe, we’re building the infrastructure layer that powers the AI-infused workplace of the future.”
Founded in 2021, Shakers is a workforce orchestration platform that connects businesses with specialised tech freelancers and autonomous AI agents. Founded by four serial entrepreneurs – Héctor Mata (CEO), Nico de Luis (COO), Adrián de Pedro (CPO) and Jaime Castillo (CRO) – with backgrounds in AI and software development, and first-hand experience in both finding freelance work and hiring talent.
With over 10,000 vetted freelancers and 450+ client companies including Microsoft, Inditex and Telefónica, Shakers aims to empower a new generation of performance-driven freeworkers with integrated services such as banking, insurance and productivity tools – all within its all-in-one Freelancer OS.
The company has grown over 6x in revenue over the past year, and is aiming to continue on its growth trajectory. 60% of their revenue reportedly comes from recurring customers and 30% of revenue generated organically from other European markets.
Shakers envisions a future of work where businesses can easily onboard specialised teams of tech freelancers and autonomous AI agents to collaborate, experiment, and drive new technological capabilities via complex and long-term projects. The platform empowers businesses and freelancers to automate their most “frustrating” workflow challenges and upgrade their productivity skills, promoting quick and effective results.
According to data provided by Shakers, this comes at a time when the traditional IT outsourcing landscape is being reshaped, with the European market projected to grow from €180 billion to over €280 billion by 2030 as demand shifts toward more flexible, high-skill talent solutions.
Shakers plans to double its team size in the next year to facilitate their growth, while expanding its AI agent integrations for freelancers and businesses. By 2027, Shakers will reportedly have orchestrated over 10,000 hybrid projects.
Simone Riva, Partech Senior Principal, said: “At Partech, we believe that while AI is reshaping the way we work, exceptional talent will always be at the heart of the future of work. From our very first conversation, it was clear the Shakers team is building something special — a fast, intuitive platform tailored to the flexible workforce of tomorrow. They’re redefining how companies find, hire, and collaborate with top talent. We’re proud to back this visionary team and are excited to support them as they scale across Spain, Europe, and beyond.”
As part of Shakers’ community of 10,000+ tech freelancers, they will enjoy the same benefits as traditional employment, including embedded services for banking, insurance, productivity and more, alongside the flexibility, freedom and incentives of self-employment.
According to Shakers, their technology goes beyond just sourcing – it validates talent end-to-end, assessing both hard and soft skills, experience, and cultural fit through more than 1,000 data points.
Integrated with Shakers Verified, their pre-vetting tool, the platform eliminates the need for early interviews and technical tests, delivering ready-to-work talent from day one. This means that companies can potentially go from idea to fully assembled team in minutes.

Youtube Placeholder
Company: OpenAI  
Revenue: US$12bn  
Employees: 2,659  
CEO: Sam Altman  
Founded: 2015
As the leader of OpenAI, Sam is one of AI’s pioneers, transforming AI from research curiosity to mainstream technology through ChatGPT’s public release.
His transition from Y Combinator president to AI evangelist positioned him as the public face of the artificial general intelligence (AGI) movement, while his fundraising success – including a USUS$40bn Series F round in March 2025 – demonstrates the capital requirements for frontier AI development.
His influence operates through both technical advancement and public narrative, creating market demand for AI applications  while securing the funding needed to build them.
evidence.raw_content:
List
People & Skills
Top 10: AI Leaders
ByKitty Wheeler
November 05, 2025
undefined mins
Share
Share

AI Magazine highlights some of the most inspirational AI leaders across the world
AI leaders are shaping the now and future of AI through infrastructure, innovation, research, funding, public perception and regulations
The AI industry is crystallising around a handful of leaders whose decisions ripple across global markets.
From the researchers who laid the theoretical groundwork decades ago to the entrepreneurs building today’s commercial platforms, these individuals lead the direction of one of technology’s most transformative sectors.
Their influence extends beyond their companies to shaping regulatory discussions, investment flows and public perception of AI’s potential and risks.
While some wield power through breakthrough research that becomes tomorrow’s products, others command the resources and vision to turn abstract possibilities into tools used by millions.
Together, they represent the concentrated influence that defines how humanity’s relationship with AI will unfold.
10. Chris Bedi
Chris Bedit, CCO and Special Advisor to the Chairman for AI Transformation | Credit: ServiceNow
Company: AI Transformation  
Revenue: US$10.98bn  
Employees: 26,293  
CEO: Bill McDermott  
Founded: 2004
As Chief Customer Officer and Special Advisor to the Chairman for AI Transformation, Chris is dedicated to helping customers harness the power of AI. He served as ServiceNow’s CDIO from 2015 to 2024, leading its AI journey.
Today, his focus is on guiding customers through their AI and Gen AI implementations and creating faster time to value.
An industry thought leader on practical AI/ML adoption in the enterprise, Chris works closely with many of the world’s leading CIOs to address key industry issues.
9. Andrew Ng
Andrew Ng, Founder of LandingAI | Credit: Getty
Company: LandingAI  
Revenue: US$19.3m  
Employees: 112  
CEO: Dan Maloney  
Founded: 2017
While Dan Maloney is the CEO of LandingAI, Andrew is the founder.
Andrew’s career spans both academia and industry, from co-founding Google Brain to serving as Baidu’s Chief Scientist.
Through Coursera, he has educated millions in AI concepts, democratising access to machine learning (ML) knowledge.
His current focus on “agentic workflows” at LandingAI targets manufacturing applications, proving that AI deployment extends beyond consumer applications into industrial processes.
Today, his vision of AI agents represents the next evolution beyond today’s chatbots.
8. Elon Musk
Elon Musk, CEO of xAI
Company: xAI  
Revenue: US$500m  
Employees: 1,200  
CEO: Elon Musk  
Founded: 2023
Elon’s entry into AI through xAI represents his attempt to create a “truth-seeking” alternative to existing large language models (LLMs).
As a co-founder of OpenAI, he now invests in AI across other companies he owns such as Tesla and SpaceX.
From developing the Neuralink which develops brain-computer interfaces using advanced AI to providing unique training data with X, his influence resonates across multiple markets.
xAI’s USUS$6bn fundraising demonstrates investor confidence in his track record, though xAI faces intense competition from established players with multi-year head starts.
7. Aravind Srinivas
Aravind Srinivas, Co-Founder and CEO of Perplexity
Company: Perplexity  
Revenue: US$148M  
Employees: 1,222  
CEO: Aravind Srinivas  
Founded:  2022
Perplexity’s fast funding growth from zero to USUS$1.22bn demonstrates investor appetite for alternatives to ChatGPT.
Perplexity stands out as its conversational search engine provides cited answers, addressing concerns about AI hallucination and misinformation.
Aravind embodies the new generation of AI entrepreneurs by challenging established players with focused applications.
His former OpenAI background also gives him insider knowledge of LLM development.
6. Yann LeCun
Yann LeCun, Chief Scientist of Meta AI | Credit: Getty
Company: Meta  
Revenue: US$47.5bn  
Employees: 75,945  
CEO: Mark Zuckerberg  
Founded: 2004
Yann’s convolutional neural networks revolutionised image recognition and earned him the Turing Award (Nobel Prize of Computing).
At Meta, he shapes AI strategy for platforms used by billions while publicly challenging apocalyptic AI predictions.
His work at AT&T Bell Labs demonstrated commercial viability by processing 10% of US bank cheques by 1998, proving AI’s business applications decades before the current boom.
Furthermore, his contrarian stance on AI safety puts him at odds with other industry leaders.
5. Mustafa Suleyman
Mustafa Suleyman, CEO of Microsoft AI
Company: Microsoft AI  
Revenue: US$76.4bn  
Employees: 228,000  
CEO: Mustafa Suleyman  
Founded: 2016
Mustafa’s move from DeepMind to Microsoft signals the industry’s talent wars as tech giants recruit top researchers.
His focus on “AI for people” through Microsoft Copilot also mirrors the shift from research to consumer applications.
His warnings about “psychosis risk” – where users might believe AI systems are conscious – highlight the psychological challenges beyond technical considerations that he addresses.
Mustafa’s experience building DeepMind gives him unique insight into scaling AI organisations and managing commercial transitions.
4. Dario Amodei
Dario Amodei, CEO of Anthropic | Credit: Getty
Company: Anthropic  
Revenue: US$4bn  
Employees: 1,097  
CEO: Dario Amodei  
Founded: 2021
Dario’s departure from OpenAI to found Anthropic created one of the industry’s most credible alternatives to ChatGPT.
His focus on AI safety and alignment attracted USUS$27.3bn in funding, demonstrating that safety-focused development can command premium valuations.
Anthropic’s constitutional AI approach is a different philosophy about AI development focusing on what is ethical.
Dario’s research background also provides technical credibility for safety claims, making him a key voice in responsible AI development discussions.
3. Demis Hassabis
Demis Hassabis, CEO of Google DeepMind | Credit: Dan Kitwood
Company: Google DeepMind  
Revenue: N/A  
Employees: 2,125  
CEO: Demis Hassabis  
Founded: 2010
Building DeepMind into Google’s AI research, Demis has created a powerhouse through breakthrough achievements like AlphaGo and AlphaFold.
The 2023 merger of DeepMind with Google Brain consolidated Alphabet’s AI efforts under his leadership, creating a research organisation capable of competing with OpenAI.
His neuroscience background and game design experience inform DeepMind’s approach to general intelligence, while Google’s resources provide the computational infrastructure needed for large-scale experiments.
2. Jensen Huang
Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia
Company: Nvidia  
Revenue: US$130.5bn  
Employees: 36,000  
CEO: Jensen Huang  
Founded: 1993
Jensen transformed Nvidia from a graphics company into the AI industry’s infrastructure provider – through the strategic development of CUDA parallel computing platforms –  a decision that would prove prescient when deep learning emerged decades later.
His early bet on enabling GPUs for general-purpose computing seemed like a risky diversification from gaming hardware, but it positioned Nvidia perfectly for the AI boom.
Today, his company’s GPUs have become the new oil powering AI development, with virtually every major AI breakthrough depending on Nvidia’s hardware architecture.
This positioning has made Nvidia one of the world’s most valuable companies, with its market capitalisation rivaling entire countries’ GDPs.
Jensen’s influence extends beyond hardware sales with his technical decisions about GPU architecture, memory systems and software platforms determine what AI applications become feasible for the entire industry.
The symbiotic relationship between AI software ambitions and Nvidia’s hardware capabilities creates a feedback loop where new AI possibilities drive demand for more powerful chips, which in turn enable even more ambitious AI projects.
1. Sam Altman
Youtube Placeholder
Company: OpenAI  
Revenue: US$12bn  
Employees: 2,659  
CEO: Sam Altman  
Founded: 2015
As the leader of OpenAI, Sam is one of AI’s pioneers, transforming AI from research curiosity to mainstream technology through ChatGPT’s public release.
His transition from Y Combinator president to AI evangelist positioned him as the public face of the artificial general intelligence (AGI) movement, while his fundraising success – including a USUS$40bn Series F round in March 2025 – demonstrates the capital requirements for frontier AI development.
His influence operates through both technical advancement and public narrative, creating market demand for AI applications  while securing the funding needed to build them.
Sam’s vision of AI creating new career paths, including speculation about graduates working in space, shows the evolving scope he envisions for the technology.
The symbiotic relationship between his market-making vision and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang’s hardware infrastructure defines the current AI ecosystem – where software ambitions drive hardware demand in an unprecedented cycle of technological and financial innovation.

Content has been added to your Folio
Artificial Intelligence (AI)
Leadership, Innovation, and the Future of AI: Lessons from Trend Micro CEO & Co-Founder Eva Chen
Discover how AI is reshaping cybersecurity through our CEO, Eva Chen’s industry briefing series. Gain practical strategies, real-world insights, and a clear roadmap to secure your AI initiatives with confidence.
By: Ashley Savoie
Aug 22, 2025
Read time:  ( words)
Share
Print
Save to Folio
As a woman working at Trend Micro, I’m proud to be part of a company led by Eva Chen, our visionary co-founder and CEO who has shaped the future of cybersecurity for decades. With women making up only 25-30% of the global tech workforce and fewer than 20% in leadership, her unwavering leadership stands as a powerful reminder of what’s possible. Before becoming CEO in late 2004, Eva served as executive vice president from 1988 to 1996 and as CTO from 1996 to 2004. During her longstanding tenure, she’s led the development of a series of industry firsts, from pioneering unique security products to transformative security management strategies that helped establish Trend as a global cybersecurity leader. Thanks to her leadership and visionary spirit, Trend has always stayed ahead of the curve, spotting tech shifts before anyone else, and working to proactively protect them. Just as we predicted how virtualization, cloud, and mobile would reshape security, Eva is now sharing her vision on how AI will transform our industry.
At Trend Micro, we have been using AI to enhance our technology for over 20 years, a track record that predates the existence of many other cybersecurity companies. That long history with AI reflects Eva’s forward-looking approach, as years ago she spoke of her dream for proactive security; a vision where threats could be anticipated and prevented rather than merely detected and remediated. Today, that dream has become reality, as AI enables truly proactive protection and proves her consistent, unwavering vision and ability to see the future of cybersecurity long before it arrives.
Introducing Eva Chen’s AI-Focused Industry Briefing Series
Over the last few months, Eva has released a series of industry briefings focused on how AI is redefining cybersecurity and the way businesses operate. The series is designed to help organizations understand both the promise and the risks of AI, while offering practical, research-backed guidance on how to secure AI systems effectively. Reading these briefings, you’ll gain insights into adversarial AI, AI governance, and practical security strategies straight from our CEO and Trend experts. If you’re a business or security leader who wants to build AI initiatives on a secure foundation, this series is for you.
The Intelligent Stack: Securing AI in Modern Software Architecture
Part one of the series, this industry briefing examines the evolution of the software stack that’s being driven by the explosion of AI, the new cybersecurity challenges that come with it, and the strategic considerations leaders must weigh to ensure their organizations can harness AI’s potential securely, responsibly, and competitively.
Reimagining Cybersecurity and Business Risk with Information Flow Digital Twins
This briefing explores how digital twin technology, enhanced by AI agents and threat intelligence, can bridge the gap between risk awareness and risk readiness. It looks at the driving forces behind this shift, the practical use cases already emerging, and what it takes to begin the journey toward a more secure, resilient future.
Secure AI Factory: Protection with Speed and Precision
In the final part of the series, explore the AI factory concept and learn why securing the entire AI lifecycle is critical. Gain insights from Trend Micro’s global threat intelligence and frontline experience, including Pwn2Own™ Berlin, the first AI-focused hacking competition, to see the real risks facing AI today. Reading this briefing, you’ll discover how a secure-by-design platform can reduce risk, simplify compliance, build trust, and speed up the value of your AI investments.
AI is advancing faster than any previous technology wave, creating both exciting opportunities and serious security challenges. This rapid evolution is driven by breakthroughs in computing power, access to massive datasets, and the widespread adoption of AI across every industry, accelerating innovation at an unprecedented pace. For organizations, this speed creates massive opportunity, but also drives significant risks, as gaps in security, governance, and understanding can leave AI systems vulnerable to attacks and misuse.
Navigating these fast-moving challenges requires businesses to rely on strong leadership and proven expertise to manage risks and fully harness AI’s potential; and Eva embodies the visionary leadership needed to navigate this moment. As one of the few women leading a global cybersecurity powerhouse, she inspires confidence in women across tech while fostering a culture that values diverse perspectives and guides teams through uncertainty. Trend’s long history of anticipating change, including investing early in cloud security and recognizing IoT and mobile threats before they became mainstream, proves this vision is more than intuition; it’s a disciplined commitment to curiosity and the courage to lead.
As a woman in tech, it’s inspiring to be part of a company where leadership is visionary, inclusive, and unafraid to take bold steps, qualities that Eva embodies as she guides us into the AI era. I encourage you to explore the AI-focused industry briefing series to gain insights from Trend’s expertise, understand the risks and opportunities of AI, and see firsthand how thoughtful leadership can help secure the future of technology.
Authors
Ashley Savoie
Director, Product Management

Héctor Mata, Shakers CEO, said: “The convergence of AI and independent talent is not a trend – it’s a new operating model. Demand for specialised technical talent is soaring, but the traditional outsourcing model hasn’t evolved in over decades. With Shakers, we are setting a new standard for hiring: our platform acts as the new operating system for performance-driven freelancers — giving them the same benefits as traditional roles, while offering companies the agility to innovate faster. As we expand across Europe, we’re building the infrastructure layer that powers the AI-infused workplace of the future.” [...] Simone Riva, Partech Senior Principal, said: “At Partech, we believe that while AI is reshaping the way we work, exceptional talent will always be at the heart of the future of work. From our very first conversation, it was clear the Shakers team is building something special — a fast, intuitive platform tailored to the flexible workforce of tomorrow. They’re redefining how companies find, hire, and collaborate with top talent. We’re proud to back this visionary team and are excited to support them as they scale across Spain, Europe, and beyond.” [...] The company has grown over 6x in revenue over the past year, and is aiming to continue on its growth trajectory. 60% of their revenue reportedly comes from recurring customers and 30% of revenue generated organically from other European markets.
Shakers envisions a future of work where businesses can easily onboard specialised teams of tech freelancers and autonomous AI agents to collaborate, experiment, and drive new technological capabilities via complex and long-term projects. The platform empowers businesses and freelancers to automate their most “frustrating” workflow challenges and upgrade their productivity skills, promoting quick and effective results.

HomeFundingMadrid-based startup Shakers raises €14 million to help businesses integrate teams of...
FundingSpain-Startups
Madrid-based startup Shakers raises €14 million to help businesses integrate teams of specialised talent with AI
By David Cendon Garcia
Share
FacebookTwitterWhatsAppLinkedin
HRTech startup Shakers, a Spanish workforce management platform connecting companies with tech freelancers, today announced its €14 million Series A funding round in order to expand double its headcount.
The funding round was led by Partech, with further investment from KFund, Brighteye Ventures, Athos Capital and Wayra.
Héctor Mata, Shakers CEO, said: “The convergence of AI and independent talent is not a trend – it’s a new operating model. Demand for specialised technical talent is soaring, but the traditional outsourcing model hasn’t evolved in over decades. With Shakers, we are setting a new standard for hiring: our platform acts as the new operating system for performance-driven freelancers — giving them the same benefits as traditional roles, while offering companies the agility to innovate faster. As we expand across Europe, we’re building the infrastructure layer that powers the AI-infused workplace of the future.”
Founded in 2021, Shakers is a workforce orchestration platform that connects businesses with specialised tech freelancers and autonomous AI agents. Founded by four serial entrepreneurs – Héctor Mata (CEO), Nico de Luis (COO), Adrián de Pedro (CPO) and Jaime Castillo (CRO) – with backgrounds in AI and software development, and first-hand experience in both finding freelance work and hiring talent.
With over 10,000 vetted freelancers and 450+ client companies including Microsoft, Inditex and Telefónica, Shakers aims to empower a new generation of performance-driven freeworkers with integrated services such as banking, insurance and productivity tools – all within its all-in-one Freelancer OS.
The company has grown over 6x in revenue over the past year, and is aiming to continue on its growth trajectory. 60% of their revenue reportedly comes from recurring customers and 30% of revenue generated organically from other European markets.
Shakers envisions a future of work where businesses can easily onboard specialised teams of tech freelancers and autonomous AI agents to collaborate, experiment, and drive new technological capabilities via complex and long-term projects. The platform empowers businesses and freelancers to automate their most “frustrating” workflow challenges and upgrade their productivity skills, promoting quick and effective results.
According to data provided by Shakers, this comes at a time when the traditional IT outsourcing landscape is being reshaped, with the European market projected to grow from €180 billion to over €280 billion by 2030 as demand shifts toward more flexible, high-skill talent solutions.
Shakers plans to double its team size in the next year to facilitate their growth, while expanding its AI agent integrations for freelancers and businesses. By 2027, Shakers will reportedly have orchestrated over 10,000 hybrid projects.
Simone Riva, Partech Senior Principal, said: “At Partech, we believe that while AI is reshaping the way we work, exceptional talent will always be at the heart of the future of work. From our very first conversation, it was clear the Shakers team is building something special — a fast, intuitive platform tailored to the flexible workforce of tomorrow. They’re redefining how companies find, hire, and collaborate with top talent. We’re proud to back this visionary team and are excited to support them as they scale across Spain, Europe, and beyond.”
As part of Shakers’ community of 10,000+ tech freelancers, they will enjoy the same benefits as traditional employment, including embedded services for banking, insurance, productivity and more, alongside the flexibility, freedom and incentives of self-employment.
According to Shakers, their technology goes beyond just sourcing – it validates talent end-to-end, assessing both hard and soft skills, experience, and cultural fit through more than 1,000 data points.
Integrated with Shakers Verified, their pre-vetting tool, the platform eliminates the need for early interviews and technical tests, delivering ready-to-work talent from day one. This means that companies can potentially go from idea to fully assembled team in minutes.

supporting_evidence.raw_content:
List
People & Skills
Top 10: AI Leaders
ByKitty Wheeler
November 05, 2025
undefined mins
Share
Share

AI Magazine highlights some of the most inspirational AI leaders across the world
AI leaders are shaping the now and future of AI through infrastructure, innovation, research, funding, public perception and regulations
The AI industry is crystallising around a handful of leaders whose decisions ripple across global markets.
From the researchers who laid the theoretical groundwork decades ago to the entrepreneurs building today’s commercial platforms, these individuals lead the direction of one of technology’s most transformative sectors.
Their influence extends beyond their companies to shaping regulatory discussions, investment flows and public perception of AI’s potential and risks.
While some wield power through breakthrough research that becomes tomorrow’s products, others command the resources and vision to turn abstract possibilities into tools used by millions.
Together, they represent the concentrated influence that defines how humanity’s relationship with AI will unfold.
10. Chris Bedi
Chris Bedit, CCO and Special Advisor to the Chairman for AI Transformation | Credit: ServiceNow
Company: AI Transformation  
Revenue: US$10.98bn  
Employees: 26,293  
CEO: Bill McDermott  
Founded: 2004
As Chief Customer Officer and Special Advisor to the Chairman for AI Transformation, Chris is dedicated to helping customers harness the power of AI. He served as ServiceNow’s CDIO from 2015 to 2024, leading its AI journey.
Today, his focus is on guiding customers through their AI and Gen AI implementations and creating faster time to value.
An industry thought leader on practical AI/ML adoption in the enterprise, Chris works closely with many of the world’s leading CIOs to address key industry issues.
9. Andrew Ng
Andrew Ng, Founder of LandingAI | Credit: Getty
Company: LandingAI  
Revenue: US$19.3m  
Employees: 112  
CEO: Dan Maloney  
Founded: 2017
While Dan Maloney is the CEO of LandingAI, Andrew is the founder.
Andrew’s career spans both academia and industry, from co-founding Google Brain to serving as Baidu’s Chief Scientist.
Through Coursera, he has educated millions in AI concepts, democratising access to machine learning (ML) knowledge.
His current focus on “agentic workflows” at LandingAI targets manufacturing applications, proving that AI deployment extends beyond consumer applications into industrial processes.
Today, his vision of AI agents represents the next evolution beyond today’s chatbots.
8. Elon Musk
Elon Musk, CEO of xAI
Company: xAI  
Revenue: US$500m  
Employees: 1,200  
CEO: Elon Musk  
Founded: 2023
Elon’s entry into AI through xAI represents his attempt to create a “truth-seeking” alternative to existing large language models (LLMs).
As a co-founder of OpenAI, he now invests in AI across other companies he owns such as Tesla and SpaceX.
From developing the Neuralink which develops brain-computer interfaces using advanced AI to providing unique training data with X, his influence resonates across multiple markets.
xAI’s USUS$6bn fundraising demonstrates investor confidence in his track record, though xAI faces intense competition from established players with multi-year head starts.
7. Aravind Srinivas
Aravind Srinivas, Co-Founder and CEO of Perplexity
Company: Perplexity  
Revenue: US$148M  
Employees: 1,222  
CEO: Aravind Srinivas  
Founded:  2022
Perplexity’s fast funding growth from zero to USUS$1.22bn demonstrates investor appetite for alternatives to ChatGPT.
Perplexity stands out as its conversational search engine provides cited answers, addressing concerns about AI hallucination and misinformation.
Aravind embodies the new generation of AI entrepreneurs by challenging established players with focused applications.
His former OpenAI background also gives him insider knowledge of LLM development.
6. Yann LeCun
Yann LeCun, Chief Scientist of Meta AI | Credit: Getty
Company: Meta  
Revenue: US$47.5bn  
Employees: 75,945  
CEO: Mark Zuckerberg  
Founded: 2004
Yann’s convolutional neural networks revolutionised image recognition and earned him the Turing Award (Nobel Prize of Computing).
At Meta, he shapes AI strategy for platforms used by billions while publicly challenging apocalyptic AI predictions.
His work at AT&T Bell Labs demonstrated commercial viability by processing 10% of US bank cheques by 1998, proving AI’s business applications decades before the current boom.
Furthermore, his contrarian stance on AI safety puts him at odds with other industry leaders.
5. Mustafa Suleyman
Mustafa Suleyman, CEO of Microsoft AI
Company: Microsoft AI  
Revenue: US$76.4bn  
Employees: 228,000  
CEO: Mustafa Suleyman  
Founded: 2016
Mustafa’s move from DeepMind to Microsoft signals the industry’s talent wars as tech giants recruit top researchers.
His focus on “AI for people” through Microsoft Copilot also mirrors the shift from research to consumer applications.
His warnings about “psychosis risk” – where users might believe AI systems are conscious – highlight the psychological challenges beyond technical considerations that he addresses.
Mustafa’s experience building DeepMind gives him unique insight into scaling AI organisations and managing commercial transitions.
4. Dario Amodei
Dario Amodei, CEO of Anthropic | Credit: Getty
Company: Anthropic  
Revenue: US$4bn  
Employees: 1,097  
CEO: Dario Amodei  
Founded: 2021
Dario’s departure from OpenAI to found Anthropic created one of the industry’s most credible alternatives to ChatGPT.
His focus on AI safety and alignment attracted USUS$27.3bn in funding, demonstrating that safety-focused development can command premium valuations.
Anthropic’s constitutional AI approach is a different philosophy about AI development focusing on what is ethical.
Dario’s research background also provides technical credibility for safety claims, making him a key voice in responsible AI development discussions.
3. Demis Hassabis
Demis Hassabis, CEO of Google DeepMind | Credit: Dan Kitwood
Company: Google DeepMind  
Revenue: N/A  
Employees: 2,125  
CEO: Demis Hassabis  
Founded: 2010
Building DeepMind into Google’s AI research, Demis has created a powerhouse through breakthrough achievements like AlphaGo and AlphaFold.
The 2023 merger of DeepMind with Google Brain consolidated Alphabet’s AI efforts under his leadership, creating a research organisation capable of competing with OpenAI.
His neuroscience background and game design experience inform DeepMind’s approach to general intelligence, while Google’s resources provide the computational infrastructure needed for large-scale experiments.
2. Jensen Huang
Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia
Company: Nvidia  
Revenue: US$130.5bn  
Employees: 36,000  
CEO: Jensen Huang  
Founded: 1993
Jensen transformed Nvidia from a graphics company into the AI industry’s infrastructure provider – through the strategic development of CUDA parallel computing platforms –  a decision that would prove prescient when deep learning emerged decades later.
His early bet on enabling GPUs for general-purpose computing seemed like a risky diversification from gaming hardware, but it positioned Nvidia perfectly for the AI boom.
Today, his company’s GPUs have become the new oil powering AI development, with virtually every major AI breakthrough depending on Nvidia’s hardware architecture.
This positioning has made Nvidia one of the world’s most valuable companies, with its market capitalisation rivaling entire countries’ GDPs.
Jensen’s influence extends beyond hardware sales with his technical decisions about GPU architecture, memory systems and software platforms determine what AI applications become feasible for the entire industry.
The symbiotic relationship between AI software ambitions and Nvidia’s hardware capabilities creates a feedback loop where new AI possibilities drive demand for more powerful chips, which in turn enable even more ambitious AI projects.
1. Sam Altman
Youtube Placeholder
Company: OpenAI  
Revenue: US$12bn  
Employees: 2,659  
CEO: Sam Altman  
Founded: 2015
As the leader of OpenAI, Sam is one of AI’s pioneers, transforming AI from research curiosity to mainstream technology through ChatGPT’s public release.
His transition from Y Combinator president to AI evangelist positioned him as the public face of the artificial general intelligence (AGI) movement, while his fundraising success – including a USUS$40bn Series F round in March 2025 – demonstrates the capital requirements for frontier AI development.
His influence operates through both technical advancement and public narrative, creating market demand for AI applications  while securing the funding needed to build them.
Sam’s vision of AI creating new career paths, including speculation about graduates working in space, shows the evolving scope he envisions for the technology.
The symbiotic relationship between his market-making vision and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang’s hardware infrastructure defines the current AI ecosystem – where software ambitions drive hardware demand in an unprecedented cycle of technological and financial innovation.

Madrid-based startup Shakers raises €14 million to help businesses integrate teams of specialised talent with AI
By David Cendon Garcia
Share
FacebookTwitterWhatsAppLinkedin
HRTech startup Shakers, a Spanish workforce management platform connecting companies with tech freelancers, today announced its €14 million Series A funding round in order to expand double its headcount.
The funding round was led by Partech, with further investment from KFund, Brighteye Ventures, Athos Capital and Wayra.
Héctor Mata, Shakers CEO, said: “The convergence of AI and independent talent is not a trend – it’s a new operating model. Demand for specialised technical talent is soaring, but the traditional outsourcing model hasn’t evolved in over decades. With Shakers, we are setting a new standard for hiring: our platform acts as the new operating system for performance-driven freelancers — giving them the same benefits as traditional roles, while offering companies the agility to innovate faster. As we expand across Europe, we’re building the infrastructure layer that powers the AI-infused workplace of the future.”
Founded in 2021, Shakers is a workforce orchestration platform that connects businesses with specialised tech freelancers and autonomous AI agents. Founded by four serial entrepreneurs – Héctor Mata (CEO), Nico de Luis (COO), Adrián de Pedro (CPO) and Jaime Castillo (CRO) – with backgrounds in AI and software development, and first-hand experience in both finding freelance work and hiring talent.
With over 10,000 vetted freelancers and 450+ client companies including Microsoft, Inditex and Telefónica, Shakers aims to empower a new generation of performance-driven freeworkers with integrated services such as banking, insurance and productivity tools – all within its all-in-one Freelancer OS.
The company has grown over 6x in revenue over the past year, and is aiming to continue on its growth trajectory. 60% of their revenue reportedly comes from recurring customers and 30% of revenue generated organically from other European markets.
Shakers envisions a future of work where businesses can easily onboard specialised teams of tech freelancers and autonomous AI agents to collaborate, experiment, and drive new technological capabilities via complex and long-term projects. The platform empowers businesses and freelancers to automate their most “frustrating” workflow challenges and upgrade their productivity skills, promoting quick and effective results.
According to data provided by Shakers, this comes at a time when the traditional IT outsourcing landscape is being reshaped, with the European market projected to grow from €180 billion to over €280 billion by 2030 as demand shifts toward more flexible, high-skill talent solutions.
Shakers plans to double its team size in the next year to facilitate their growth, while expanding its AI agent integrations for freelancers and businesses. By 2027, Shakers will reportedly have orchestrated over 10,000 hybrid projects.
Simone Riva, Partech Senior Principal, said: “At Partech, we believe that while AI is reshaping the way we work, exceptional talent will always be at the heart of the future of work. From our very first conversation, it was clear the Shakers team is building something special — a fast, intuitive platform tailored to the flexible workforce of tomorrow. They’re redefining how companies find, hire, and collaborate with top talent. We’re proud to back this visionary team and are excited to support them as they scale across Spain, Europe, and beyond.”
As part of Shakers’ community of 10,000+ tech freelancers, they will enjoy the same benefits as traditional employment, including embedded services for banking, insurance, productivity and more, alongside the flexibility, freedom and incentives of self-employment.
According to Shakers, their technology goes beyond just sourcing – it validates talent end-to-end, assessing both hard and soft skills, experience, and cultural fit through more than 1,000 data points.
Integrated with Shakers Verified, their pre-vetting tool, the platform eliminates the need for early interviews and technical tests, delivering ready-to-work talent from day one. This means that companies can potentially go from idea to fully assembled team in minutes.

supporting_evidence.snippet:

Yann LeCun
VP & Chief AI Scientist at Meta
Tags
AIAI LeadersAI InfluencersAI ExecutivesAI CEO's [...] Sam’s vision of AI creating new career paths, including speculation about graduates working in space, shows the evolving scope he envisions for the technology.
The symbiotic relationship between his market-making vision and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang’s hardware infrastructure defines the current AI ecosystem – where software ambitions drive hardware demand in an unprecedented cycle of technological and financial innovation.
